questions.question: Why can't a video VLM just feed every frame to the LLM?
options: Video codecs are proprietary
A 1-minute clip at 30 FPS is 1800 frames; at 196 tokens per frame that's 352k tokens — larger than any 2024-era LLM context, so frames must be subsampled, pooled, or Q-former-compressed
Frames arrive faster than GPUs can copy
LLMs only accept square inputs
questions.explanation: Each reduction has its own tax: subsampling loses temporal detail, pooling loses spatial detail, and a Q-former (16-frame clip → 64 tokens) loses a little of both but saves the most tokens.

questions.question: What was Video-LLaMA's architecture, and what was its key strength and weakness?
options: Per-pixel attention over raw video

16-frame clips at 2 FPS through a Video Q-former (32 queries) plus a parallel ImageBind audio branch — strength: audio-visual joint reasoning; weakness: fixed clip length with no arbitrary time grounding
A diffusion model that denoises captions
Pure text training on subtitle files
questions.explanation: Video-LLaMA (June 2023) was the first open video-LLM. VideoChat dropped the audio branch; Video-LLaVA unified image and video frames in one encoder — but all stayed 8-16-frame systems.

questions.question: What makes TMRoPE different from simple temporal embeddings?
options: It uses learned position tables per video
Each patch token carries (t, h, w) where t is the ABSOLUTE timestamp, not a frame index — per-token rotation that natively handles uneven dynamic-FPS spacing
It encodes time in the color channels
It only works at exactly 30 FPS
questions.explanation: The model sees "at 4.2 seconds," not "at frame 15." That's what enables precise grounding answers like "the cat jumps at 4.2 seconds" where Video-LLaMA could only say "early in the clip."

questions.question: How do the three temporal-token designs the lesson compares differ?
options: They differ only in training data

Q-former-per-clip (Video-LLaMA: 16 frames → 32 queries), pooled-per-frame (Video-LLaVA: per-frame tokens with learned temporal embeddings), and M-RoPE-per-token (Qwen2.5-VL: every token rotated by its timestamp)
All three pool frames identically
Only one of them can process video at all
questions.explanation: The granularity of temporal information rises left to right — clip-level, frame-level, token-level — and grounding accuracy rises with it, independent of the vision encoder.

questions.question: Which benchmarks does the lesson name for evaluating video-language models?
options: MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, ARC
VideoMME, TempCompass, EgoSchema, and Video-MMMU
ImageNet, COCO, VQAv2, OK-VQA
LibriSpeech, CommonVoice, FLEURS
questions.explanation: These four stress different skills — general video QA, temporal-order sensitivity, long egocentric reasoning, and knowledge-intensive video understanding — so a sampler/encoding choice that helps one may not help another.
